symbols: lion lying with the lamb — the messianic peace where predators and prey coexist (ISA.11.6)
child among wild animals — the innocence and safety of the Kingdom of God
diverse animals together — the reconciliation of all creation under the Messiah's reign
Penn's treaty in the background — historical peace-making as a type of the eschatological vision
text inscriptions from Isaiah — direct scriptural framing of the painted vision
description: A composition divided between a foreground scene of animals and children and a background scene of historical figures. In the foreground, a lion, a leopard, a bear, an ox, and other animals peacefully coexist alongside small children. A child reaches toward or rests against the lion. In the background at left, a group of European settlers and Native Americans gather in what appears to be a treaty scene. The painting is framed with text inscriptions. The style is naive or folk art with flat, decorative forms and a warm palette.
theological_description: Edward Hicks's Peaceable Kingdom is one of the most iconic visual interpretations of Isaiah's messianic prophecy: 'The wolf shall dwell with the lamb, and the leopard shall lie down with the young goat, and the calf and the lion and the fattened calf together; and a little child shall lead them' (ISA.11.6). This vision of cosmic reconciliation describes the restoration of Eden's harmony under the reign of the Messiah, the 'shoot from the stump of Jesse' upon whom the Spirit of the LORD rests (ISA.11.1-2). The original curse that set enmity between creatures (GEN.3.15) is reversed in the messianic age. Paul echoes this hope when he writes that 'the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God' (ROM.8.21). The inclusion of Penn's treaty with the Native Americans in the background reflects Hicks's Quaker belief that the peaceable kingdom could be partially realized in human history through justice and non-violence. The child leading the animals recalls Jesus's teaching: 'Unless you turn and become like children, you will never enter the kingdom of heaven' (MAT.18.3).

scripture_refs.reason: The messianic prophecy of the Peaceable Kingdom directly illustrated in this painting
The shoot from Jesse's stump upon whom the Spirit rests, the Messiah who inaugurates this peace
Creation set free from bondage to corruption, the cosmic scope of the Peaceable Kingdom
The curse on creation after the Fall, reversed in Isaiah's messianic vision
Unless you become like children — the child leading the animals embodies this teaching
